structure_blocks.heading: Method of payment
structure_blocks.text: Method of payment

structure_blocks.heading: Method of payment
structure_blocks.text: The method of payment is the way Canada will pay for work performed or goods delivered, as all arrears (preferred), in advance, as a lot delivery or as each item are delivered. This section provides guidelines for determining the method of payment, including special considerations for multi-fiscal year contracts and international purchases.

structure_blocks.heading: Determining method of payment
structure_blocks.text: Determining method of payment

structure_blocks.heading: Determining method of payment
structure_blocks.text: Choosing the appropriate method of payment allows you to manage finances responsibly, manage risks, and comply with Canada's financial policies.

structure_blocks.heading: Determining method of payment
structure_blocks.text: The most appropriate method of payment must be determined based on the particular procurement. Here are some key elements to consider.

structure_blocks.heading: Risk assessment
structure_blocks.text: Risk assessment

structure_blocks.heading: Risk assessment
structure_blocks.text: Evaluate the financial risks to Canada in scenarios like insolvency, or cancellation of, or default on work. Assess the marketability and potential resale value of work-in-progress against the total of progress payments made by Canada to measure the risk. The disparity between the amount of progress payments and the resale value of inventory is a measure of the risk exposure for Canada.

structure_blocks.heading: Financing cost estimates
structure_blocks.text: Financing cost estimates

structure_blocks.heading: Financing cost estimates
structure_blocks.text: For advance or progress payments, calculate the associated financing costs by applying the chartered bank prime lending rate to the cumulative net amount to be financed (i.e. the cumulative cash payout by Canada, less the cumulative value of item deliveries) based on realistic estimates of work progress and delivery timelines.

structure_blocks.heading: Price reduction evaluation
structure_blocks.text: Price reduction evaluation

structure_blocks.heading: Price reduction evaluation
structure_blocks.text: Consider how the method of payment could influence the overall contract price. Advance or progress payments, which reduce the contractor's need for external financing, are expected to result in cost savings for Canada.

structure_blocks.heading: Financial capacity assessment
structure_blocks.text: Financial capacity assessment

structure_blocks.heading: Financial capacity assessment
structure_blocks.text: Confirm that the client possesses adequate funds to support the selected method of payment.

structure_blocks.heading: Types of method of payment
structure_blocks.text: Types of method of payment

structure_blocks.heading: Types of method of payment
structure_blocks.text: Various methods of payment can be applied depending on the contract's specifics and the client's financial situation. The following are examples of methods of payment.

structure_blocks.heading: Acquisition card
structure_blocks.text: Acquisition card

structure_blocks.heading: Acquisition card
structure_blocks.text: The intent of acquisition cards is to provide a convenient and less burdensome method of procuring and paying for goods and services, while ensuring effective financial control. It is government policy to use acquisition cards for departmental procurement and payment of goods and services (within the levels of procurement authority delegated to departments) where it is efficient, economical and operationally feasible to do so. This policy applies to all organizations considered to be departments within the meaning of section 2 of the Financial Administration Act .

structure_blocks.heading: Acquisition card
structure_blocks.text: See Treasury Board (TB) Directive on Payments for more information on payment by acquisition cards.

structure_blocks.heading: Acquisition card
structure_blocks.text: The acquisition card has been in the forefront of most procurement and payment process improvements in client departments. The increasing use of acquisition cards has had an impact on the traditional paper based control framework. The Office of the Auditor General conducted a government-wide review of acquisition card usage, and the report stressed the need for better controls and better monitoring by departments.

structure_blocks.heading: Acquisition card
structure_blocks.text: "Corporate acquisition cards" are designed to help eliminate paper in the procurement process. These cards allow an employee to charge purchases for which full payment is made by the department to the applicable card provider. There is no fee for the cards and conditional discounts can be obtained in exchange for faster and/or electronic settlement.

structure_blocks.heading: Acquisition card
structure_blocks.text: Like a personal credit card:

structure_blocks.heading: Acquisition card
structure_blocks.text: the cardholder receives a monthly statement listing purchases

structure_blocks.heading: Acquisition card
structure_blocks.text: each cardholder is assigned a credit limit, and

structure_blocks.heading: Acquisition card
structure_blocks.text: suppliers receive 97 to 98.5 percent of the purchase price within two days of purchase

structure_blocks.heading: Acquisition card
structure_blocks.text: Unlike a personal credit card:

structure_blocks.heading: Acquisition card
structure_blocks.text: the department is obligated to pay either individual invoices for each card or one "consolidated" bill summarizing monthly purchases made by all cardholders

structure_blocks.heading: Acquisition card
structure_blocks.text: there is a defined maximum liability of $50 to the department in the event of fraudulent use

structure_blocks.heading: Acquisition card
structure_blocks.text: there is special application of financial authorities, and

structure_blocks.heading: Acquisition card
structure_blocks.text: a department can obtain access to all card transactions

structure_blocks.heading: Acquisition card
structure_blocks.text: The card is issued in the name of the employee designated to do the purchasing; however, the liability rests with the sponsoring department. Most important, the dollar amount of purchases and monthly limits associated with acquisition cards are controlled by the card-using organization.

structure_blocks.heading: Acquisition card
structure_blocks.text: The use of acquisition cards within federal government departments and agencies offers very significant opportunities for savings in the procurement through payment process. Clients can reduce and simplify the procurement process of goods and services using acquisition cards. Local purchase orders and petty cash are eliminated and a single payment is made for multiple purchases. The ease and flexibility of using the cards provide an incentive to purchase only as the need arises rather than buying in bulk. By using an acquisition card to make the purchase and then settling these purchases electronically, departments can realize significant savings. Taken across government, this approach allows for major cost avoidance in the procurement process.

structure_blocks.heading: Single payment
structure_blocks.text: Single payment

structure_blocks.heading: Single payment
structure_blocks.text: Appropriate for contracts where payment is issued upon the complete fulfillment of work and deliveries. This method demands comprehensive verification to ensure all contractual obligations are met before payment is disbursed.

structure_blocks.heading: Multiple payments
structure_blocks.text: Multiple payments

structure_blocks.heading: Multiple payments
structure_blocks.text: Suitable for contracts with multiple delivery points, where payments are made following the completion of each delivery phase. This method helps manage cash flow effectively.

structure_blocks.heading: Special considerations for foreign purchases
structure_blocks.text: Special considerations for foreign purchases

structure_blocks.heading: Special considerations for foreign purchases
structure_blocks.text: In the case of United States purchases, progress payments or other payments on account have an effect on the application of taxes, relating to the time and place of ownership being transferred to Canada. Legal Services should be consulted to ensure that appropriate terms in the contract protect against unnecessary taxes.

structure_blocks.heading: Special considerations for foreign purchases
structure_blocks.text: For other foreign purchases, where progress payments or other payments on account are granted, a check should be made to determine if the application of sales, use, or some other form of tax is related to the time and place of ownership being transferred to Canada. If this is the case, Legal Services must be consulted.

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: Progress or advanced payments

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: Recommended for contracts requiring significant initial investment or those of a prolonged nature. Payments are disbursed as predetermined milestones are achieved.

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: In all cases payments must be aligned with the fiscal year in which goods or services are received. Additionally, the following conditions must be met:

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: provision of adequate security for the payment

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: assurance that the value Canada receives aligns with the payment amount, and

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: verification that the client’s budget can accommodate the financing needs

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: And at least one of the following circumstances exists:

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: there is economic advantage to Canada that clearly outweighs the financing cost associated with the progress payment or advance payment

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: the contractor may face financial hardship or only secure financing with difficulty or at high, uneconomical rates compared to current bank prime lending rates

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: the contract's value is deemed to exceed the contractor's assessed financial capabilities

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: the contract has a long performance duration, or there is an established tradition or practice in the industry of receiving progress or advance payments from the purchaser

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: Note:

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: Contracts with long durations or in industries with traditions of advance payments may require tailored payment schedules to match project milestones or industry norms

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: In the case of subscriptions or insurance premiums, which are often for a term of one full year and which may not start exactly on April 1, payments can be restricted to goods or services provided in no more than the current and next fiscal years. For instance, a publication subscription paid in February 2009 cannot cover a period beyond March 2010

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: For multi-year contracts, buyers can negotiate the payment of a series of separate advances covering each fiscal year. Thus, a payment can be made for a maintenance contract, for the period of a contract, from February to March 2009, and then another payment covering the period from April 2009 to March 2010

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: In the extremely rare and exceptional circumstance, such as armament purchases or extended warranty service, where up-front payments covering more than one fiscal year are required the buyer can determine if an advance payment is unavoidable and can be substantiated

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: In the case of a progress payment, the ownership of the materials or work-in-process will be transferred to Canada upon making such payment.

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: When a progress payment is to be used; milestones, when possible, should be specified to relate payments to measurable progress on the contract. Technical or other contractual achievement yardsticks may be used as milestones. Milestone payment is a form of progress payment addressed under the policy related to progress payments. The value of each milestone should be negotiated before contract award.

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: When progress payments against milestones are not possible because of the nature of the contract, progress payments may be made at set periods of time on a calendar basis (time payment method), or based upon the actual costs incurred for material purchases and the partial completion of work, as certified by company and government inspectors.

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: A combination of milestone and cost incurred progress payments is also possible for different phases of the contract. The combination method can be used, for example, to pay incurred costs in the early stages of a major procurement when it would be difficult to define milestones, with payments for later and more definable stages of the production process made against specified milestone achievements.

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: If milestone or cost incurred progress payments are not possible, the time payment method of making progress payments should be used with caution. The overriding requirement for use of this method is the existence of a project progress monitoring and control system, to provide the contracting officer with reliable indicators of the actual value of work accomplished when a payment is due. With the exception of rental and service contracts, the time payment method must be approved at the director level or above.

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: This section outlines the circumstances under which advance payments should be considered, aligning with Treasury Board (TB ) guidelines and specific considerations for different contract values and contexts. It also addresses the requirements for guarantees, cash discounts and special conditions related to foreign purchases.

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: You may consider advance payments only under extraordinary circumstances. Your decision process and requirements will vary based on the nature of the contract and it’s value.

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: For contracts for services exceeding $25,000 :

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: a form of guarantee from a financially strong third party is necessary to protect any advance payment

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: the guarantee usually takes the form of a surety bond from an associated or parent company or a financial institution, or an irrevocable letter of credit from a Canadian bank. It should provide for return to Canada of the unliquidated balance of the advance, plus interest, in the event of work cancellation or other contract termination for Canada’s convenient. Other types of guarantees may be discussed with a cost analyst

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: the need for guarantees can be waived only with a strong business case

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: For contracts for services valued up to $25,000:

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: you may waive the requirement for security, if the contractor has a positive industry reputation, and where no significant financial or performance issues have been recorded in past engagements with Canada

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: For all contracts, except those for advertising, payment may be made in advance of the due date when the contractor offers a cash discount for advance payment and the discount at least offsets the cost to Canada for early payment.

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: Note: Cash discounts are not to be considered in the evaluation of offers.

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: In the context of purchases from the government of the United States (US) through the Foreign Military Sales (FMS) program, advance payments are mandated by US law prior to the commencement of delivery for goods and services to a foreign-based contractor. In this case, Treasury Board has approved the standard conditions for FMS sales from the US Government. For any deviation from standard conditions for these sales, you must seek approval from the Treasury Board.

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: Progress payments play a crucial role in managing contract finances, providing a structured approach to paying for work as it progresses.

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: The following guidance outlines the different types of progress payments. It provides information on how to use them effectively to ensure alignment with government policies and contract requirements.

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: Key characteristics include:

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: Ownership transfer: Making a progress payment results in the transfer of ownership for materials or work-in-progress to Canada, according to specified general contract conditions.

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: Milestone payments : This form of progress payments involves payments associated with clearly defined milestones, aligning them with measurable contract progress. You should establish and agree on these milestones, acting as technical or contractual benchmarks, before awarding the contract. For contracts involving milestone payments against a set schedule, you should utilize specified clauses and forms, incorporating holdback conditions as applicable.

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: Alternative methods of payment : When milestone payments are not feasible, you should consider progress payments based on set time periods or actual costs incurred for materials and partial work completion, verified through inspections. Various clauses are available for different types of contracts, including those for goods, services and maintenance, to facilitate payments based on incurred costs or work completion over time.

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: Combining methods of payment: It is possible to blend milestone and cost-incurred payment strategies for different phases of a contract. This approach is particularly useful for managing payments in the initial stages of procurement or when later stages are marked by clear milestones.

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: Fixed interval payments: To be used with caution. When using progress payments based on the passage of time, you must monitor diligently to ensure payments reflect the actual value of work completed. This method must be approved at the director-level, with the exception of rental and service contracts, where monthly payments are the standard.

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: Holdbacks are vital for mitigating risks of overpayment and encouraging contract completion. You are required to integrate holdbacks in contracts with progress payments, but you may exercise discretion for contracts with milestone payments.

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: Contracts involving progress payments adhere to distinct limits on allowable costs, purchases of materials and profit calculations. These limits vary depending on types of contract.

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: Contract: Firm price with milestone payments | Total allowable costs: Up to 100% of negotiated milestones | Purchased accountable advance materials: Nil | Goods and services tax/harmonized sales tax: Nil | Profit: Nil

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: Contract: Firm price with progress payment based on negotiated cost | Total allowable costs: Up to 90% | Purchased accountable advance materials: 100% | Goods and services tax/harmonized sales tax: If payable | Profit: Pro rata

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: Contract: Cost reimbursable | Total allowable costs: Up to 90% | Purchased accountable advance materials: 100% | Goods and services tax/harmonized sales tax: If payable | Profit: Pro rata

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: Contract: Fixed time rate | Total allowable costs: Up to 90% | Purchased accountable advance materials: 100% | Goods and services tax/harmonized sales tax: If payable | Profit: Pro rata

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: Contract: Price to be negotiated – Based on previous year’s rates/prices | Total allowable costs: Up to 100% | Purchased accountable advance materials: 100% | Goods and services tax/harmonized sales tax: If payable | Profit: Pro rata

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: Contract: Prices to be negotiated – all other contracts | Total allowable costs: Up to 75% | Purchased accountable advance materials: 100% | Goods and services tax/harmonized sales tax: If payable | Profit: Pro rata

structure_blocks.heading: Progress or advanced payments
structure_blocks.text: You may consider exceptions to these payment ceilings under certain conditions, such as when trade practices support exceptions, for organizations not receiving profit or fee, or when alternative financial protections are employed , for example, security deposits (government guaranteed bonds, bills of exchange, irrevocable standby letters of credit) or surety bonds .

structure_blocks.heading: Deciding on a method of payment
heading_path: Deciding on a method of payment
structure_blocks.text: Deciding on a method of payment

structure_blocks.heading: Deciding on a method of payment
heading_path: Deciding on a method of payment
structure_blocks.text: The timing for making decisions relating to the method of payment to be used varies with the solicitation method employed:

structure_blocks.heading: Deciding on a method of payment
heading_path: Deciding on a method of payment
structure_blocks.text: For an Invitation to Tender (ITT) , the method of payment must be selected, before issuing and included in the solicitation documents. Financing costs will not constitute an evaluation factor

structure_blocks.heading: Deciding on a method of payment
heading_path: Deciding on a method of payment
structure_blocks.text: For competitive solicitations , the solicitation will clearly specify that any requirement on the part of the supplier for receipt of progress or advance payments will constitute an evaluation criterion. When evaluating offers, the cost to Canada of providing the progress payments or advance payments will be taken into account, as will the risk of exposure from the method of payment, and the availability of funds

raw_text:
Acquisition card management StartFragment Progress payments Advance payments Criteria for advance payments Cash discount considerations Special considerations for foreign purchases How to use progress payments Implementing progress payments Holdbacks Specifying limits on payments for contracts involving progress payments Payment limits based on contract type

Method of payment

The method of payment is the way Canada will pay for work performed or goods delivered, as all arrears (preferred), in advance, as a lot delivery or as each item are delivered. This section provides guidelines for determining the method of payment, including special considerations for multi-fiscal year contracts and international purchases.

Determining method of payment

Choosing the appropriate method of payment allows you to manage finances responsibly, manage risks, and comply with Canada's financial policies.

The most appropriate method of payment must be determined based on the particular procurement. Here are some key elements to consider.

Risk assessment

Evaluate the financial risks to Canada in scenarios like insolvency, or cancellation of, or default on work. Assess the marketability and potential resale value of work-in-progress against the total of progress payments made by Canada to measure the risk. The disparity between the amount of progress payments and the resale value of inventory is a measure of the risk exposure for Canada.

Financing cost estimates

For advance or progress payments, calculate the associated financing costs by applying the chartered bank prime lending rate to the cumulative net amount to be financed (i.e. the cumulative cash payout by Canada, less the cumulative value of item deliveries) based on realistic estimates of work progress and delivery timelines.

Price reduction evaluation

Consider how the method of payment could influence the overall contract price. Advance or progress payments, which reduce the contractor's need for external financing, are expected to result in cost savings for Canada.

Financial capacity assessment

Confirm that the client possesses adequate funds to support the selected method of payment.

Types of method of payment

Various methods of payment can be applied depending on the contract's specifics and the client's financial situation. The following are examples of methods of payment.

Acquisition card

The intent of acquisition cards is to provide a convenient and less burdensome method of procuring and paying for goods and services, while ensuring effective financial control. It is government policy to use acquisition cards for departmental procurement and payment of goods and services (within the levels of procurement authority delegated to departments) where it is efficient, economical and operationally feasible to do so. This policy applies to all organizations considered to be departments within the meaning of section 2 of the Financial Administration Act .

See Treasury Board (TB) Directive on Payments for more information on payment by acquisition cards.

The acquisition card has been in the forefront of most procurement and payment process improvements in client departments. The increasing use of acquisition cards has had an impact on the traditional paper based control framework. The Office of the Auditor General conducted a government-wide review of acquisition card usage, and the report stressed the need for better controls and better monitoring by departments.

"Corporate acquisition cards" are designed to help eliminate paper in the procurement process. These cards allow an employee to charge purchases for which full payment is made by the department to the applicable card provider. There is no fee for the cards and conditional discounts can be obtained in exchange for faster and/or electronic settlement.

Like a personal credit card:

the cardholder receives a monthly statement listing purchases

each cardholder is assigned a credit limit, and

suppliers receive 97 to 98.5 percent of the purchase price within two days of purchase

Unlike a personal credit card:

the department is obligated to pay either individual invoices for each card or one "consolidated" bill summarizing monthly purchases made by all cardholders

there is a defined maximum liability of $50 to the department in the event of fraudulent use

there is special application of financial authorities, and

a department can obtain access to all card transactions

The card is issued in the name of the employee designated to do the purchasing; however, the liability rests with the sponsoring department. Most important, the dollar amount of purchases and monthly limits associated with acquisition cards are controlled by the card-using organization.

The use of acquisition cards within federal government departments and agencies offers very significant opportunities for savings in the procurement through payment process. Clients can reduce and simplify the procurement process of goods and services using acquisition cards. Local purchase orders and petty cash are eliminated and a single payment is made for multiple purchases. The ease and flexibility of using the cards provide an incentive to purchase only as the need arises rather than buying in bulk. By using an acquisition card to make the purchase and then settling these purchases electronically, departments can realize significant savings. Taken across government, this approach allows for major cost avoidance in the procurement process.

Single payment

Appropriate for contracts where payment is issued upon the complete fulfillment of work and deliveries. This method demands comprehensive verification to ensure all contractual obligations are met before payment is disbursed.

Multiple payments

Suitable for contracts with multiple delivery points, where payments are made following the completion of each delivery phase. This method helps manage cash flow effectively.

Special considerations for foreign purchases

In the case of United States purchases, progress payments or other payments on account have an effect on the application of taxes, relating to the time and place of ownership being transferred to Canada. Legal Services should be consulted to ensure that appropriate terms in the contract protect against unnecessary taxes.

For other foreign purchases, where progress payments or other payments on account are granted, a check should be made to determine if the application of sales, use, or some other form of tax is related to the time and place of ownership being transferred to Canada. If this is the case, Legal Services must be consulted.

Progress or advanced payments

Recommended for contracts requiring significant initial investment or those of a prolonged nature. Payments are disbursed as predetermined milestones are achieved.

In all cases payments must be aligned with the fiscal year in which goods or services are received. Additionally, the following conditions must be met:

provision of adequate security for the payment

assurance that the value Canada receives aligns with the payment amount, and

verification that the client’s budget can accommodate the financing needs

And at least one of the following circumstances exists:

there is economic advantage to Canada that clearly outweighs the financing cost associated with the progress payment or advance payment

the contractor may face financial hardship or only secure financing with difficulty or at high, uneconomical rates compared to current bank prime lending rates

the contract's value is deemed to exceed the contractor's assessed financial capabilities

the contract has a long performance duration, or there is an established tradition or practice in the industry of receiving progress or advance payments from the purchaser

Note:

Contracts with long durations or in industries with traditions of advance payments may require tailored payment schedules to match project milestones or industry norms

In the case of subscriptions or insurance premiums, which are often for a term of one full year and which may not start exactly on April 1, payments can be restricted to goods or services provided in no more than the current and next fiscal years. For instance, a publication subscription paid in February 2009 cannot cover a period beyond March 2010

For multi-year contracts, buyers can negotiate the payment of a series of separate advances covering each fiscal year. Thus, a payment can be made for a maintenance contract, for the period of a contract, from February to March 2009, and then another payment covering the period from April 2009 to March 2010

In the extremely rare and exceptional circumstance, such as armament purchases or extended warranty service, where up-front payments covering more than one fiscal year are required the buyer can determine if an advance payment is unavoidable and can be substantiated

In the case of a progress payment, the ownership of the materials or work-in-process will be transferred to Canada upon making such payment.

When a progress payment is to be used; milestones, when possible, should be specified to relate payments to measurable progress on the contract. Technical or other contractual achievement yardsticks may be used as milestones. Milestone payment is a form of progress payment addressed under the policy related to progress payments. The value of each milestone should be negotiated before contract award.

When progress payments against milestones are not possible because of the nature of the contract, progress payments may be made at set periods of time on a calendar basis (time payment method), or based upon the actual costs incurred for material purchases and the partial completion of work, as certified by company and government inspectors.

A combination of milestone and cost incurred progress payments is also possible for different phases of the contract. The combination method can be used, for example, to pay incurred costs in the early stages of a major procurement when it would be difficult to define milestones, with payments for later and more definable stages of the production process made against specified milestone achievements.

If milestone or cost incurred progress payments are not possible, the time payment method of making progress payments should be used with caution. The overriding requirement for use of this method is the existence of a project progress monitoring and control system, to provide the contracting officer with reliable indicators of the actual value of work accomplished when a payment is due. With the exception of rental and service contracts, the time payment method must be approved at the director level or above.

This section outlines the circumstances under which advance payments should be considered, aligning with Treasury Board (TB ) guidelines and specific considerations for different contract values and contexts. It also addresses the requirements for guarantees, cash discounts and special conditions related to foreign purchases.

You may consider advance payments only under extraordinary circumstances. Your decision process and requirements will vary based on the nature of the contract and it’s value.

For contracts for services exceeding $25,000 :

a form of guarantee from a financially strong third party is necessary to protect any advance payment

the guarantee usually takes the form of a surety bond from an associated or parent company or a financial institution, or an irrevocable letter of credit from a Canadian bank. It should provide for return to Canada of the unliquidated balance of the advance, plus interest, in the event of work cancellation or other contract termination for Canada’s convenient. Other types of guarantees may be discussed with a cost analyst

the need for guarantees can be waived only with a strong business case

For contracts for services valued up to $25,000:

you may waive the requirement for security, if the contractor has a positive industry reputation, and where no significant financial or performance issues have been recorded in past engagements with Canada

For all contracts, except those for advertising, payment may be made in advance of the due date when the contractor offers a cash discount for advance payment and the discount at least offsets the cost to Canada for early payment.

Note: Cash discounts are not to be considered in the evaluation of offers.

In the context of purchases from the government of the United States (US) through the Foreign Military Sales (FMS) program, advance payments are mandated by US law prior to the commencement of delivery for goods and services to a foreign-based contractor. In this case, Treasury Board has approved the standard conditions for FMS sales from the US Government. For any deviation from standard conditions for these sales, you must seek approval from the Treasury Board.

Progress payments play a crucial role in managing contract finances, providing a structured approach to paying for work as it progresses.

The following guidance outlines the different types of progress payments. It provides information on how to use them effectively to ensure alignment with government policies and contract requirements.

Key characteristics include:

Ownership transfer: Making a progress payment results in the transfer of ownership for materials or work-in-progress to Canada, according to specified general contract conditions.

Milestone payments : This form of progress payments involves payments associated with clearly defined milestones, aligning them with measurable contract progress. You should establish and agree on these milestones, acting as technical or contractual benchmarks, before awarding the contract. For contracts involving milestone payments against a set schedule, you should utilize specified clauses and forms, incorporating holdback conditions as applicable.

Alternative methods of payment : When milestone payments are not feasible, you should consider progress payments based on set time periods or actual costs incurred for materials and partial work completion, verified through inspections. Various clauses are available for different types of contracts, including those for goods, services and maintenance, to facilitate payments based on incurred costs or work completion over time.

Combining methods of payment: It is possible to blend milestone and cost-incurred payment strategies for different phases of a contract. This approach is particularly useful for managing payments in the initial stages of procurement or when later stages are marked by clear milestones.

Fixed interval payments: To be used with caution. When using progress payments based on the passage of time, you must monitor diligently to ensure payments reflect the actual value of work completed. This method must be approved at the director-level, with the exception of rental and service contracts, where monthly payments are the standard.

Holdbacks are vital for mitigating risks of overpayment and encouraging contract completion. You are required to integrate holdbacks in contracts with progress payments, but you may exercise discretion for contracts with milestone payments.

Contracts involving progress payments adhere to distinct limits on allowable costs, purchases of materials and profit calculations. These limits vary depending on types of contract.

Contract: Firm price with milestone payments | Total allowable costs: Up to 100% of negotiated milestones | Purchased accountable advance materials: Nil | Goods and services tax/harmonized sales tax: Nil | Profit: Nil

Contract: Firm price with progress payment based on negotiated cost | Total allowable costs: Up to 90% | Purchased accountable advance materials: 100% | Goods and services tax/harmonized sales tax: If payable | Profit: Pro rata

Contract: Cost reimbursable | Total allowable costs: Up to 90% | Purchased accountable advance materials: 100% | Goods and services tax/harmonized sales tax: If payable | Profit: Pro rata

Contract: Fixed time rate | Total allowable costs: Up to 90% | Purchased accountable advance materials: 100% | Goods and services tax/harmonized sales tax: If payable | Profit: Pro rata

Contract: Price to be negotiated – Based on previous year’s rates/prices | Total allowable costs: Up to 100% | Purchased accountable advance materials: 100% | Goods and services tax/harmonized sales tax: If payable | Profit: Pro rata

Contract: Prices to be negotiated – all other contracts | Total allowable costs: Up to 75% | Purchased accountable advance materials: 100% | Goods and services tax/harmonized sales tax: If payable | Profit: Pro rata

You may consider exceptions to these payment ceilings under certain conditions, such as when trade practices support exceptions, for organizations not receiving profit or fee, or when alternative financial protections are employed , for example, security deposits (government guaranteed bonds, bills of exchange, irrevocable standby letters of credit) or surety bonds .

Deciding on a method of payment

The timing for making decisions relating to the method of payment to be used varies with the solicitation method employed:

For an Invitation to Tender (ITT) , the method of payment must be selected, before issuing and included in the solicitation documents. Financing costs will not constitute an evaluation factor

For competitive solicitations , the solicitation will clearly specify that any requirement on the part of the supplier for receipt of progress or advance payments will constitute an evaluation criterion. When evaluating offers, the cost to Canada of providing the progress payments or advance payments will be taken into account, as will the risk of exposure from the method of payment, and the availability of funds
markdown_text:
Acquisition card management StartFragment Progress payments Advance payments Criteria for advance payments Cash discount considerations Special considerations for foreign purchases How to use progress payments Implementing progress payments Holdbacks Specifying limits on payments for contracts involving progress payments Payment limits based on contract type

Method of payment

The method of payment is the way Canada will pay for work performed or goods delivered, as all arrears (preferred), in advance, as a lot delivery or as each item are delivered. This section provides guidelines for determining the method of payment, including special considerations for multi-fiscal year contracts and international purchases.

Determining method of payment

Choosing the appropriate method of payment allows you to manage finances responsibly, manage risks, and comply with Canada's financial policies.

The most appropriate method of payment must be determined based on the particular procurement. Here are some key elements to consider.

Risk assessment

Evaluate the financial risks to Canada in scenarios like insolvency, or cancellation of, or default on work. Assess the marketability and potential resale value of work-in-progress against the total of progress payments made by Canada to measure the risk. The disparity between the amount of progress payments and the resale value of inventory is a measure of the risk exposure for Canada.

Financing cost estimates

For advance or progress payments, calculate the associated financing costs by applying the chartered bank prime lending rate to the cumulative net amount to be financed (i.e. the cumulative cash payout by Canada, less the cumulative value of item deliveries) based on realistic estimates of work progress and delivery timelines.

Price reduction evaluation

Consider how the method of payment could influence the overall contract price. Advance or progress payments, which reduce the contractor's need for external financing, are expected to result in cost savings for Canada.

Financial capacity assessment

Confirm that the client possesses adequate funds to support the selected method of payment.

Types of method of payment

Various methods of payment can be applied depending on the contract's specifics and the client's financial situation. The following are examples of methods of payment.

Acquisition card

The intent of acquisition cards is to provide a convenient and less burdensome method of procuring and paying for goods and services, while ensuring effective financial control. It is government policy to use acquisition cards for departmental procurement and payment of goods and services (within the levels of procurement authority delegated to departments) where it is efficient, economical and operationally feasible to do so. This policy applies to all organizations considered to be departments within the meaning of section 2 of the Financial Administration Act .

See Treasury Board (TB) Directive on Payments for more information on payment by acquisition cards.

The acquisition card has been in the forefront of most procurement and payment process improvements in client departments. The increasing use of acquisition cards has had an impact on the traditional paper based control framework. The Office of the Auditor General conducted a government-wide review of acquisition card usage, and the report stressed the need for better controls and better monitoring by departments.

"Corporate acquisition cards" are designed to help eliminate paper in the procurement process. These cards allow an employee to charge purchases for which full payment is made by the department to the applicable card provider. There is no fee for the cards and conditional discounts can be obtained in exchange for faster and/or electronic settlement.

Like a personal credit card:

the cardholder receives a monthly statement listing purchases

each cardholder is assigned a credit limit, and

suppliers receive 97 to 98.5 percent of the purchase price within two days of purchase

Unlike a personal credit card:

the department is obligated to pay either individual invoices for each card or one "consolidated" bill summarizing monthly purchases made by all cardholders

there is a defined maximum liability of $50 to the department in the event of fraudulent use

there is special application of financial authorities, and

a department can obtain access to all card transactions

The card is issued in the name of the employee designated to do the purchasing; however, the liability rests with the sponsoring department. Most important, the dollar amount of purchases and monthly limits associated with acquisition cards are controlled by the card-using organization.

The use of acquisition cards within federal government departments and agencies offers very significant opportunities for savings in the procurement through payment process. Clients can reduce and simplify the procurement process of goods and services using acquisition cards. Local purchase orders and petty cash are eliminated and a single payment is made for multiple purchases. The ease and flexibility of using the cards provide an incentive to purchase only as the need arises rather than buying in bulk. By using an acquisition card to make the purchase and then settling these purchases electronically, departments can realize significant savings. Taken across government, this approach allows for major cost avoidance in the procurement process.

Single payment

Appropriate for contracts where payment is issued upon the complete fulfillment of work and deliveries. This method demands comprehensive verification to ensure all contractual obligations are met before payment is disbursed.

Multiple payments

Suitable for contracts with multiple delivery points, where payments are made following the completion of each delivery phase. This method helps manage cash flow effectively.

Special considerations for foreign purchases

In the case of United States purchases, progress payments or other payments on account have an effect on the application of taxes, relating to the time and place of ownership being transferred to Canada. Legal Services should be consulted to ensure that appropriate terms in the contract protect against unnecessary taxes.

For other foreign purchases, where progress payments or other payments on account are granted, a check should be made to determine if the application of sales, use, or some other form of tax is related to the time and place of ownership being transferred to Canada. If this is the case, Legal Services must be consulted.

Progress or advanced payments

Recommended for contracts requiring significant initial investment or those of a prolonged nature. Payments are disbursed as predetermined milestones are achieved.

In all cases payments must be aligned with the fiscal year in which goods or services are received. Additionally, the following conditions must be met:

provision of adequate security for the payment

assurance that the value Canada receives aligns with the payment amount, and

verification that the client’s budget can accommodate the financing needs

And at least one of the following circumstances exists:

there is economic advantage to Canada that clearly outweighs the financing cost associated with the progress payment or advance payment

the contractor may face financial hardship or only secure financing with difficulty or at high, uneconomical rates compared to current bank prime lending rates

the contract's value is deemed to exceed the contractor's assessed financial capabilities

the contract has a long performance duration, or there is an established tradition or practice in the industry of receiving progress or advance payments from the purchaser

Note:

Contracts with long durations or in industries with traditions of advance payments may require tailored payment schedules to match project milestones or industry norms

In the case of subscriptions or insurance premiums, which are often for a term of one full year and which may not start exactly on April 1, payments can be restricted to goods or services provided in no more than the current and next fiscal years. For instance, a publication subscription paid in February 2009 cannot cover a period beyond March 2010

For multi-year contracts, buyers can negotiate the payment of a series of separate advances covering each fiscal year. Thus, a payment can be made for a maintenance contract, for the period of a contract, from February to March 2009, and then another payment covering the period from April 2009 to March 2010

In the extremely rare and exceptional circumstance, such as armament purchases or extended warranty service, where up-front payments covering more than one fiscal year are required the buyer can determine if an advance payment is unavoidable and can be substantiated

In the case of a progress payment, the ownership of the materials or work-in-process will be transferred to Canada upon making such payment.

When a progress payment is to be used; milestones, when possible, should be specified to relate payments to measurable progress on the contract. Technical or other contractual achievement yardsticks may be used as milestones. Milestone payment is a form of progress payment addressed under the policy related to progress payments. The value of each milestone should be negotiated before contract award.

When progress payments against milestones are not possible because of the nature of the contract, progress payments may be made at set periods of time on a calendar basis (time payment method), or based upon the actual costs incurred for material purchases and the partial completion of work, as certified by company and government inspectors.

A combination of milestone and cost incurred progress payments is also possible for different phases of the contract. The combination method can be used, for example, to pay incurred costs in the early stages of a major procurement when it would be difficult to define milestones, with payments for later and more definable stages of the production process made against specified milestone achievements.

If milestone or cost incurred progress payments are not possible, the time payment method of making progress payments should be used with caution. The overriding requirement for use of this method is the existence of a project progress monitoring and control system, to provide the contracting officer with reliable indicators of the actual value of work accomplished when a payment is due. With the exception of rental and service contracts, the time payment method must be approved at the director level or above.

This section outlines the circumstances under which advance payments should be considered, aligning with Treasury Board (TB ) guidelines and specific considerations for different contract values and contexts. It also addresses the requirements for guarantees, cash discounts and special conditions related to foreign purchases.

You may consider advance payments only under extraordinary circumstances. Your decision process and requirements will vary based on the nature of the contract and it’s value.

For contracts for services exceeding $25,000 :

a form of guarantee from a financially strong third party is necessary to protect any advance payment

the guarantee usually takes the form of a surety bond from an associated or parent company or a financial institution, or an irrevocable letter of credit from a Canadian bank. It should provide for return to Canada of the unliquidated balance of the advance, plus interest, in the event of work cancellation or other contract termination for Canada’s convenient. Other types of guarantees may be discussed with a cost analyst

the need for guarantees can be waived only with a strong business case

For contracts for services valued up to $25,000:

you may waive the requirement for security, if the contractor has a positive industry reputation, and where no significant financial or performance issues have been recorded in past engagements with Canada

For all contracts, except those for advertising, payment may be made in advance of the due date when the contractor offers a cash discount for advance payment and the discount at least offsets the cost to Canada for early payment.

Note: Cash discounts are not to be considered in the evaluation of offers.

In the context of purchases from the government of the United States (US) through the Foreign Military Sales (FMS) program, advance payments are mandated by US law prior to the commencement of delivery for goods and services to a foreign-based contractor. In this case, Treasury Board has approved the standard conditions for FMS sales from the US Government. For any deviation from standard conditions for these sales, you must seek approval from the Treasury Board.

Progress payments play a crucial role in managing contract finances, providing a structured approach to paying for work as it progresses.

The following guidance outlines the different types of progress payments. It provides information on how to use them effectively to ensure alignment with government policies and contract requirements.

Key characteristics include:

Ownership transfer: Making a progress payment results in the transfer of ownership for materials or work-in-progress to Canada, according to specified general contract conditions.

Milestone payments : This form of progress payments involves payments associated with clearly defined milestones, aligning them with measurable contract progress. You should establish and agree on these milestones, acting as technical or contractual benchmarks, before awarding the contract. For contracts involving milestone payments against a set schedule, you should utilize specified clauses and forms, incorporating holdback conditions as applicable.

Alternative methods of payment : When milestone payments are not feasible, you should consider progress payments based on set time periods or actual costs incurred for materials and partial work completion, verified through inspections. Various clauses are available for different types of contracts, including those for goods, services and maintenance, to facilitate payments based on incurred costs or work completion over time.

Combining methods of payment: It is possible to blend milestone and cost-incurred payment strategies for different phases of a contract. This approach is particularly useful for managing payments in the initial stages of procurement or when later stages are marked by clear milestones.

Fixed interval payments: To be used with caution. When using progress payments based on the passage of time, you must monitor diligently to ensure payments reflect the actual value of work completed. This method must be approved at the director-level, with the exception of rental and service contracts, where monthly payments are the standard.

Holdbacks are vital for mitigating risks of overpayment and encouraging contract completion. You are required to integrate holdbacks in contracts with progress payments, but you may exercise discretion for contracts with milestone payments.

Contracts involving progress payments adhere to distinct limits on allowable costs, purchases of materials and profit calculations. These limits vary depending on types of contract.

Contract: Firm price with milestone payments | Total allowable costs: Up to 100% of negotiated milestones | Purchased accountable advance materials: Nil | Goods and services tax/harmonized sales tax: Nil | Profit: Nil

Contract: Firm price with progress payment based on negotiated cost | Total allowable costs: Up to 90% | Purchased accountable advance materials: 100% | Goods and services tax/harmonized sales tax: If payable | Profit: Pro rata

Contract: Cost reimbursable | Total allowable costs: Up to 90% | Purchased accountable advance materials: 100% | Goods and services tax/harmonized sales tax: If payable | Profit: Pro rata

Contract: Fixed time rate | Total allowable costs: Up to 90% | Purchased accountable advance materials: 100% | Goods and services tax/harmonized sales tax: If payable | Profit: Pro rata

Contract: Price to be negotiated – Based on previous year’s rates/prices | Total allowable costs: Up to 100% | Purchased accountable advance materials: 100% | Goods and services tax/harmonized sales tax: If payable | Profit: Pro rata

Contract: Prices to be negotiated – all other contracts | Total allowable costs: Up to 75% | Purchased accountable advance materials: 100% | Goods and services tax/harmonized sales tax: If payable | Profit: Pro rata

You may consider exceptions to these payment ceilings under certain conditions, such as when trade practices support exceptions, for organizations not receiving profit or fee, or when alternative financial protections are employed , for example, security deposits (government guaranteed bonds, bills of exchange, irrevocable standby letters of credit) or surety bonds .

Deciding on a method of payment

The timing for making decisions relating to the method of payment to be used varies with the solicitation method employed:

For an Invitation to Tender (ITT) , the method of payment must be selected, before issuing and included in the solicitation documents. Financing costs will not constitute an evaluation factor

For competitive solicitations , the solicitation will clearly specify that any requirement on the part of the supplier for receipt of progress or advance payments will constitute an evaluation criterion. When evaluating offers, the cost to Canada of providing the progress payments or advance payments will be taken into account, as will the risk of exposure from the method of payment, and the availability of funds
